channel.title: NPR Topics: World
channel.description: NPR world news, international art and culture, world business and financial markets, world economy, and global trends in health, science and technology. Subscribe to the World Story of the Day podcast and RSS feed.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: 2 US service members missing after military exercises in Morocco
item.description: Two U.S. service members are missing in southwestern Morocco after taking part in annual multinational military exercises in the North African country, the United States Africa Command (AFRICOM) said.
item.content:encoded:
Two U.S. service members are missing in southwestern Morocco after taking part in annual multinational military exercises in the North African country, the United States Africa Command (AFRICOM) said.

(Image credit: Mosa'ab Elshamy)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Trump says the U.S will 'guide' stranded ships from the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The United States will launch an effort on Monday to "guide" stranded ships from the Iran-gripped Strait of Hormuz, President Donald Trump said, as two ships around the strait reported attacks.
item.content:encoded:
The United States will launch an effort on Monday to "guide" stranded ships from the Iran-gripped Strait of Hormuz, President Donald Trump said, as two ships around the strait reported attacks.

(Image credit: Razieh Poudat/AP)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Hantavirus outbreak kills 3 on cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean, WHO says
item.description: Hantaviruses are usually spread by exposure to urine, saliva or feces from infected rodents, such as rats or mice. Hantavirus infections are rare but can cause deadly respiratory infections.
item.content:encoded:
Hantaviruses are usually spread by exposure to urine, saliva or feces from infected rodents, such as rats or mice. Hantavirus infections are rare but can cause deadly respiratory infections.

(Image credit: AFP via Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Rob Stein

item.title: Gas prices went up more than 30 cents a gallon last week. How high could they go?
item.description: U.S. gas prices were nearly $3 an average prior to the start of the war in Iran.
item.content:encoded:
U.S. gas prices were nearly $3 an average prior to the start of the war in Iran.

(Image credit: Jenny Kane)
item.dc:creator: Chandelis Duster

item.title: Entrepreneurs fight pile-up of garbage in the West Bank
item.description: Israeli restrictions on movement in the West Bank are impeding garbage trucks from reaching landfills, leaving Palestinians living amid mounds of trash. Two Palestinian entrepreneurs are trying to change this.
item.content:encoded: Israeli restrictions on movement in the West Bank are impeding garbage trucks from reaching landfills, leaving Palestinians living amid mounds of trash. Two Palestinian entrepreneurs are trying to change this.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Beardsley

item.title: Blasting beats and neon lights: inside Nairobi's 'nganya' bus culture
item.description: In Nairobi, the matatu isn't just a ride—it's a rolling nightclub, art gallery, and heartbeat of the city all in one.
item.content:encoded: In Nairobi, the matatu isn't just a ride—it's a rolling nightclub, art gallery, and heartbeat of the city all in one.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Igunza

item.title: Modern economic chokepoints in war and the impact on geopolitics
item.description: Hoover Institution fellow Eyck Freymann explains what the Iran war and the blockade of the Straight of Hormuz reveals about modern economic chokepoints, geopolitics and war strategies.
item.content:encoded: Hoover Institution fellow Eyck Freymann explains what the Iran war and the blockade of the Straight of Hormuz reveals about modern economic chokepoints, geopolitics and war strategies.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: How does diplomacy work during a military deadlock?
item.description: Suzanne DiMaggio, senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, explains the nuances of diplomatic moves at a time of military stalemate.
item.content:encoded: Suzanne DiMaggio, senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, explains the nuances of diplomatic moves at a time of military stalemate.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: 'I just want to see her again' says son of imprisoned Myanmar ex-leader Aung San Suu Kyi
item.description: On Thursday, authorities in Myanmar claimed they had transferred Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi from prison to house arrest. Her son Kim Aris spoke to NPR about his doubts about the regime's account.
item.content:encoded:
On Thursday, authorities in Myanmar claimed they had transferred Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi from prison to house arrest. Her son Kim Aris spoke to NPR about his doubts about the regime's account.

(Image credit: AP)
item.dc:creator: Lorcan Lovett

item.title: Germany says U.S. troop withdrawal 'anticipated', Spain and Italy could be next
item.description: Germany's defense minister is playing down the impact of the Pentagon's decision to pull 5,000 troops from the country, but the move has rattled NATO allies and added to growing fears that Europe can no longer rely on Washington.
item.content:encoded:
Germany's defense minister is playing down the impact of the Pentagon's decision to pull 5,000 troops from the country, but the move has rattled NATO allies and added to growing fears that Europe can no longer rely on Washington.

(Image credit: Markus Schreiber)
item.dc:creator: Willem Marx
